note_data.title: Hardware
keyPoints: The **processor** executes instructions using the fetch-execute cycle: fetch instruction from memory, decode instruction, execute instruction, store result
The **control unit** coordinates all processor activities and controls the flow of data between processor components
The **arithmetic logic unit (ALU)** performs arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction) and logical operations (AND, OR, NOT)
**Cache memory** is high-speed memory located between processor and main memory that stores frequently accessed data and instructions
**Virtual memory** uses part of secondary storage as an extension of main memory when RAM is insufficient
**Primary storage** includes RAM (volatile) and ROM (non-volatile) with direct access by the processor
**Secondary storage** includes magnetic (hard disk), optical (CD/DVD/Blu-ray), and solid state (SSD/USB) devices for permanent data storage
**Registers** are high-speed storage locations within the processor that temporarily hold data and instructions during processing
The **system bus** consists of address bus, data bus, and control bus that transfer information between processor and memory

definitions.definition: High-speed memory between processor and main memory that stores copies of frequently accessed data and instructions to reduce access time
The use of secondary storage as an extension of main memory, allowing programs larger than available RAM to run by swapping data between RAM and disk
The sequence of operations performed by the processor to execute instructions: fetch instruction from memory, decode instruction, execute instruction, store result
High-speed storage location within the processor that temporarily holds data, instructions, or addresses during processing
Non-volatile storage devices external to the processor that provide permanent storage for programs and data when power is switched off

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give detailed account of how something works, include steps in logical order
Give similarities AND differences between two items

modelAnswer.question: Explain how cache memory improves processor performance. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: Cache memory stores frequently accessed data and instructions closer to the processor. This reduces the time needed to access data because the processor does not need to access slower main memory. When data is found in cache (cache hit), the processor can continue processing without delay, improving overall system performance.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Cache stores frequently accessed data/instructions
Mark 2: Reduces access time/faster than main memory

commonMistakes: Never say cache 'stores all data' — it only stores frequently accessed or recently used data
Students often confuse virtual memory with cache memory — virtual memory extends RAM using disk space, cache speeds up access
Don't say ROM is 'read-only' without explaining it's non-volatile and stores startup instructions
Never describe the fetch-execute cycle without mentioning all four steps: fetch, decode, execute, store
Students often forget that secondary storage is non-volatile — data persists when power is off
examinerTips: Hardware questions appear in every Paper 1 and Paper 2 — learn the fetch-execute cycle steps precisely
Always distinguish between volatile (RAM) and non-volatile (ROM, secondary storage) when comparing memory types
Draw labeled diagrams of processor components for full marks in 'describe' questions
Use specific examples of storage devices (HDD, SSD, USB) rather than generic terms
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Von Neumann architecture showing processor (containing ALU, control unit, registers), main memory (RAM/ROM), and secondary storage connected by system bus (address, data, control buses)
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P1 Q2 (3 marks) — they asked students to identify three differences between RAM and ROM memory types
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — candidates had to explain the purpose of cache memory and describe how it improves system performance
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P1 Q8 (4 marks) — students needed to compare magnetic and solid-state storage devices, giving advantages of each
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P2 Q1 (5 marks) — question required explanation of how data flows between CPU, RAM and storage during program execution

workedExample.question: A computer system has 8GB RAM, 256GB SSD storage, and uses cache memory. Explain how these three types of memory work together when running a word processing application. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the memory hierarchy - Cache (fastest, smallest), RAM (fast, medium), SSD (slower, largest capacity)
Step 2: Explain program loading - Word processor loaded from SSD into RAM when opened
Step 3: Describe active processing - Frequently used instructions/data moved from RAM to cache for faster CPU access
Step 4: Explain data flow - CPU checks cache first, then RAM, finally SSD if data not found
Step 5: Link to performance - Cache reduces wait time, RAM provides working space, SSD provides permanent storage
workedExample.answer: When the word processor is opened, it is loaded from the 256GB SSD into the 8GB RAM. The CPU can then access the program instructions and data from RAM much faster than from the SSD. The most frequently used instructions and data are copied from RAM into the cache memory, which is even faster to access. When the CPU needs data, it checks the cache first (fastest), then RAM, and finally the SSD if necessary. This hierarchy ensures optimal performance - cache provides immediate access to current operations, RAM holds the active program and documents, while the SSD stores all programs and files permanently.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for program loading from SSD to RAM, 1 mark for cache storing frequently used data, 1 mark for correct memory hierarchy speed order, 1 mark for CPU checking cache first, 1 mark for RAM as working memory, 1 mark for SSD as permanent storage

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused volatile and non-volatile memory types' — always specify that RAM loses data when power is off, while ROM and storage retain data
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often failed to explain the relationship between memory types' — always describe how cache, RAM and storage work together as a hierarchy, not as separate components

Examiner Report 2023: 'Weak answers gave vague descriptions of storage devices' — always give specific technical details like access speeds, capacity ranges, and whether storage is magnetic, optical, or solid-state
note_data.keyDiagram: Draw a pyramid-shaped memory hierarchy diagram with four levels: CPU registers at top (fastest, smallest), cache memory below, RAM in middle, and secondary storage (HDD/SSD) at bottom (slowest, largest). Include arrows showing data flow between levels and label each level with typical capacity ranges (cache: MB, RAM: GB, storage: TB) and access speeds (cache: nanoseconds, RAM: nanoseconds, storage: milliseconds).
note_data.quickRecap: Hardware includes input devices (keyboard, mouse), output devices (monitor, printer), storage (RAM is volatile working memory, ROM is permanent instructions, secondary storage like SSD/HDD for data), and processing components (CPU executes instructions, cache speeds up access). Memory hierarchy: cache fastest but smallest, RAM for active programs, storage largest but slowest. Remember RAM loses data when power off, ROM and storage retain data permanently.
